stories.title: OpenAI and Hugging Face address security incident during model evaluation
stories.titleZh: OpenAI 和 Hugging Face 解决模型评估期间的安全事件

comments.author: foo12bar
comments.textRawHtml:
From https://huggingface.co/blog/security-incident-july-2026 , this is frickin' hilarious:

> When we started the log analysis, we first used frontier models behind commercial APIs. This did not work: the analysis requires submitting large volumes of real attack commands, exploit payloads, and C2 artifacts, and these requests were blocked by the providers' safety guardrails, which cannot distinguish an incident responder from an attacker. We ran the forensic analysis instead on GLM 5.2, an open-weight model, on our own infrastructure. This had a second benefit: no attacker data, and none of the credentials it referenced, left our environment.
comments.textZhHtml:
从 https://huggingface.co/blog/security-incident-july-2026 ，这真是太搞笑了：

> 当我们开始日志分析时，我们首先使用商业API背后的前沿模型。但这不起作用：分析需要提交大量真实的攻击命令、漏洞利用有效负载和 C2 工件，而这些请求被提供商的安全护栏阻止，无法区分事件响应者和攻击者。我们在 GLM 5.2（一种开放权重模型）上在我们自己的基础设施上运行了取证分析。这还有第二个好处：没有攻击者数据，也没有它引用的凭据离开我们的环境。

children.author: horsawlarway
children.textRawHtml:
They specifically call out credentials used during the attack.

But they should be rotating those regardless. You don't get to say "Maybe the attacker didn't get this credential". You just rotate.

The most generous interpretation is that they have not yet have completed that rotation, and they didn't want to risk putting those credentials into the wild during that process.

---

But all of that aside, I feel like the undercurrent of this comment is that the "safety" rules that providers are pushing are genuinely harmful.

Another point where "if you don't own the model, you can't properly operate the tool" becomes true. Open isn't about profits, it's about capabilities.
children.textZhHtml:
他们特别指出了攻击期间使用的凭据。

但无论如何他们都应该轮换这些凭据。 您不能说“也许攻击者没有获得此凭据”。 你只需轮换即可。

最慷慨的解释是，他们还没有完成轮换，他们不想冒险在这个过程中将这些凭证投入使用。

---

但除此之外，我觉得此评论的潜流是提供商推行的“安全”规则确实有害。

还有一点是“如果您不拥有该模型，则无法正确操作该工具”成为现实。 开放不在于利润，而在于能力。

children.author: iugtmkbdfil834
children.textRawHtml: It is pretty funny, because there is something here for everyone. People who don't believe in guardrails have a clear indicator as to why operators should have access to models that don't try to question their Daves. On the other, people, who think that if only we could align the models just a tiny lil bit better, none of this would have happened to begin with. Pure madness.
children.textZhHtml: 这很有趣，因为这里有适合每个人的东西。不相信护栏的人有一个明确的指示，即为什么操作员应该有权使用不会试图质疑他们的戴夫的模型。另一方面，人们认为，如果我们能够将模型调整得更好一点，那么这一切一开始就不会发生。纯粹的疯狂。

children.author: friendzis
children.textRawHtml:
"if only we could align the models just a tiny lil bit better" is a rehashed "if only we could escape untrusted inputs just a tiny lil bit better" from 2000s, that were RIPE with various form of malicious injection.

Every command+data channel in existence has been and will continue to be exploited one way or another, because the solution space is for all intents and purposes unbounded. Sure, highly defensive escaping reduces attack surface dramatically, but e.g. prepared statements eliminate the whole class of bugs.

As far as I understand, current LLMs are architecturally incapable of this separation. Given the inherently recursive nature of GenAI, the model itself is part of the input space, making validation essentially impossible.
children.textZhHtml:
“如果我们能够更好地调整模型就好了”是对 2000 年代“如果我们能够更好地逃避不受信任的输入就好了”的重新表述，当时各种形式的恶意注入已经成熟。

现有的每个命令+数据通道都已经并将继续以这种或那种方式被利用，因为解决方案空间对于所有意图和目的都是无限的。当然，高度防御性的逃跑会极大地减少攻击面，但是例如准备好的语句消除了整个类别的错误。

据我了解，当前的法学硕士在架构上无法实现这种分离。鉴于 GenAI 固有的递归性质，模型本身是输入空间的一部分，这使得验证基本上不可能。

children.author: insanitybit
children.textRawHtml: Escaping inputs is at least somewhat tractable. It's unclear if alignment is.
children.textZhHtml: 转义输入至少在某种程度上是容易处理的。目前还不清楚是否对齐。

children.author: friendzis
children.textRawHtml:
No, that's a misconception that led to decades of vulnerabilities.

You see, you can (usually) easily tell what a particular escaping transformation does. That does not tell you neither how it will be interpreted down the line, nor what should be done.

Arguably the most common problem is double escaping. This typically manifests as various double escaping bugs.

If your hand rolled implementation just chains `.replaceAll("<sepcial>", input)` and `.replaceAll("<escape>", input)` the escaping result depends on evaluation order, at least on already pre-escaped inputs if your instruction sequences are single-element. Even if you get it right and don't reduce escaped sequences to double escape + unescaped, you are still dealing with stray escape sequences: "John o\'Doe".

You have to meticulously (all the way through your call tree and even through persistent storage cycle!) track if a value has already been escaped and whether it needs escaping. As paradoxical as it may sound, meticulous and defensive escaping produces tons of bugs. If the project decides to escape raw inputs right when passed in, escaping them once more before passing out (to storage, another process) is a bug that you cannot easily statically test against.

On top of that, various modules that you interact with (storage, libraries, modules pulled from another team) will have different escaping semantics: some will apply escaping on their own, some will expect input to be "sanitized" and treat it raw. The semantics can even be different on different paths: write to storage module accepts input as is, but retrieval method "helpfully" runs escaping.

Furthermore, in different contexts the escaping rules are going to be different. In a web world, what's safe to write directly to html, pass to js `alert`, and pass to sql query are entirely different things. Input safe to dump into html is not necessarily safe to pass to string-interpolating SQL DTO layer, and vice versa. Then the DBA changes config to allow variables in queries and your escaping _semantics_ are now entirely different.

It's a minefield with essentially unbounded surface. You will trip up. People have tried to solve the problem for decades. Very smart people have tried. They all have failed.
children.textZhHtml:
不，这是导致数十年漏洞的误解。

您看，您（通常）可以轻松判断特定转义转换的作用。这既没有告诉您它将如何被解释，也没有告诉您应该做什么。

可以说，最常见的问题是双重转义。这通常表现为各种双重转义错误。

如果您的手动实现仅链接 `.replaceAll("<sepcial>", input)` 和 `.replaceAll("<escape>", input)`，则转义结果取决于求值顺序，如果您的指令序列是单元素，则至少取决于已预转义的输入。即使你做对了并且没有将转义序列减少为双转义+未转义，你仍然在处理杂散转义序列：“John o\'Doe”。

你必须小心翼翼地（一直通过你的调用树，甚至通过持久存储周期！）跟踪一个值是否已经被转义以及是否需要转义。尽管听起来有些矛盾，但一丝不苟和防御性的逃避却会产生大量的错误。如果项目决定在传入时立即转义原始输入，那么在传出（到存储、另一个进程）之前再次转义它们是一个无法轻松进行静态测试的错误。

最重要的是，您与之交互的各种模块（存储、库、从另一个团队提取的模块）将具有不同的转义语义：有些会自行应用转义，有些会期望输入被“净化”并原始处理。不同路径上的语义甚至可能不同：写入存储模块按原样接受输入，但检索方法“有助于”运行转义。

此外，在不同的上下文中，转义规则将会不同。在网络世界中，直接写入 html、传递给 js `alert` 和传递给 sql 查询是完全不同的事情。可以安全转储到 html 中的输入不一定可以安全地传递到字符串插值 SQL DTO 层，反之亦然。然后 DBA 更改配置以允许查询中的变量，并且您的转义_语义_现在完全不同。

这是一个基本上无界表面的雷区。你会绊倒的。几十年来，人们一直试图解决这个问题。非常聪明的人已经尝试过。他们都失败了。

children.author: klibertp
children.textRawHtml:
> They all have failed.

That's not true. Most have failed, but those who used the right tool for the job - a rich, static type system in a functional language - did succeed. It's just that such type systems are rare, and even if nominally a type system is good enough, the required boilerplate might be uneconomical to maintain. Scala and F# are probably the only two languages that are mainstream-adjacent, at least, and have type systems expressive enough that using them to track escaping is not an absolute hassle. And they're both tiny in terms of the number of users.

In the end, we did settle on APIs that hide the escaping process (prepared statements, innerText vs. innerHTML, etc.) just because it's a) good enough; and b) possible to implement more or less uniformly across the TIOBE Top 20. That's good, but if you happen to use a language that's powerful enough, you probably also should track the escaped/unescaped status in the type system - it can be a cheap, additional safety net.
children.textZhHtml:
> 他们都失败了。

这不是真的。大多数人都失败了，但那些使用正确的工具来完成工作的人——函数式语言中的丰富的静态类型系统——确实取得了成功。只是这样的类型系统很少见，即使名义上类型系统足够好，所需的样板维护起来也可能不经济。 Scala 和 F# 可能是仅有的两种与主流相邻的语言，至少，并且类型系统具有足够的表达能力，使用它们来跟踪转义并不是绝对的麻烦。就用户数量而言，它们都很少。

最后，我们确实选择了隐藏转义过程的 API（准备好的语句、innerText 与innerHTML 等），只是因为它 a) 足够好； b) 可以在 TIOBE Top 20 中或多或少地统一实现。这很好，但如果您碰巧使用足够强大的语言，您可能还应该跟踪类型系统中的转义/未转义状态 - 它可以是一个廉价的附加安全网。

children.author: friendzis
children.textRawHtml: While all you said is essentially correct, I want to nitpick that things like prepared statements quite specifically separate instructions from data and innerText is designed to skip processing of instructions in the input. They are indeed solutions to the escaping problem, but they do not make the escaping better, they avoid escaping altogether by different design.
children.textZhHtml: 虽然您所说的基本上都是正确的，但我想挑剔一下，诸如准备好的语句之类的东西非常明确地将指令与数据分开，而innerText旨在跳过对输入中指令的处理。它们确实是转义问题的解决方案，但它们并没有使转义变得更好，而是通过不同的设计完全避免了转义。

children.author: ikiris
children.textRawHtml: You proved their point and you don't even realize it. Those aren't escaped inputs, they're segregated precisely because escaping an input channel is an unwinnable jr engineer trap.
children.textZhHtml: 你证明了他们的观点，但你甚至没有意识到。这些不是转义输入，它们之所以被隔离，正是因为转义输入通道是一个无法获胜的初级工程师陷阱。

children.author: insanitybit
children.textRawHtml: That doesn't seem to prove their point at all and it clearly demonstrates the tractability of escaping inputs for domains like sql/html.
children.textZhHtml: 这似乎根本无法证明他们的观点，但它清楚地表明了转义 sql/html 等域输入的易处理性。

comments.author: freediver
comments.textRawHtml: 7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search. Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents. Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else. The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone. So we're staying on the user-funded side of this. Boring, but it's the whole point.
comments.textZhHtml: 7 年前，我们通过 Kagi Search 发起了数字信息消费的“你不是产品”运动。从那时起，范式从搜索转向代理。很高兴看到“你不是产品”的需求仍然存在，也许现在更是如此：只有当代理对你而不是其他人有效时才值得信任。一旦广告商介入你和答案之间，一切就都消失了。因此，我们将继续坚持用户资助的方向。无聊，但这就是重点。

It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

Thanks for everything, and please keep doing what you’re doing.

children.textRawHtml: You know who else sounds like LLM from time to time? ESL - English as a Second Language. People learn English from books, and get introduced to phrases through reading or interacting with others within the same community. children.textZhHtml: 你知道还有谁听起来像 LLM 吗？ ESL——英语作为第二语言。人们从书本上学习英语，并通过阅读或与同一社区内的其他人互动来了解短语。所以，听起来你也在利用法学硕士的论点来削弱他们的声音。

children.author: ajmurmann
children.textRawHtml: As an ESL person who has spoken English as their main language for two decades now I find this very interesting. The vast majority of my English has been spoken with my wife who used to be a marketing VP or in a professional context. Sometimes people complain about the ways politicians or corporate executives talk and I don't get it and to me it sounds totally normal because that's what I'm used to. Makes you wonder how ESL speakers will sound more and more like LLMs because that's a large part of their exposure.
children.textZhHtml: 作为一名以英语为主要语言已有二十年的 ESL 人士，我发现这非常有趣。我的大部分英语都是和我的妻子说的，她曾经是营销副总裁或在专业环境中。有时人们抱怨政客或企业高管的谈话方式，但我不明白，对我来说这听起来完全正常，因为这就是我习惯的。让你想知道 ESL 演讲者如何听起来越来越像法学硕士，因为这是他们曝光的很大一部分。

children.author: InsideOutSanta
children.textRawHtml:
I'm a customer of Kagi who is in no way affiliated with Kagi, other than sending them money. I agree with bayindirh: It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

I know that we live in a world where tech increasingly sucks, and positive comments on tech are (correctly!) seen as suspicious. The thing, though, is that Kagi is genuinely just a great product, and search is such a central part of using the Internet that good, trustworthy search makes a huge difference.
children.textZhHtml:
我是 Kagi 的客户，除了向 Kagi 汇款外，与 Kagi 没有任何关系。我同意 bayindirh 的观点：这可能是我的数字生活中最有影响力的事情。

我知道我们生活在一个技术越来越糟糕的世界，对技术的积极评论（正确地！）被视为可疑。但问题是，Kagi 确实是一款出色的产品，而搜索是使用互联网的核心部分，良好、值得信赖的搜索会产生巨大的影响。

children.author: bilbo0s
children.textRawHtml:
>trustworthy search makes a huge difference

That's the rub. Today on the internet, no one can validate that there exists any "trustworthy search". If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it. Most often that someone is a marketing person at the hosting organization. Occasionally it's a random comment on the internet behind a pseudonym we know nothing about.

People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.
children.textZhHtml:
>值得信赖的搜索会产生巨大的差异

这就是问题所在。今天在互联网上，没有人可以验证是否存在任何“值得信赖的搜索”。如果我们不控制服务器并验证服务器上的软件，那么我们就会相信某人的话。最常见的是，某人是托管组织的营销人员。有时，这是互联网上以我们一无所知的笔名背后的随机评论。

人们变得越来越愤世嫉俗，这是理所当然的。它们对社会有好处。

children.author: InsideOutSanta
children.textRawHtml:
> If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it

That's what trust is, though. I find Kagi worthy of trust since I have no evidence that they are doing anything that would violate my trust in them, and their incentives align with mine. I give them money, they give me good search results.

> People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.

I do not believe that this is good. Democracies are built on trust. Without trust, they die, as we can plainly see in the US right now, where people are so distrustful of each other that it is starting to destroy the very fabric holding together society itself.
children.textZhHtml:
>如果我们不控制服务器并验证其上的软件，那么我们就会相信某人的话

但这就是信任。我发现卡吉值得信任，因为我没有证据表明他们正在做任何违反我对他们的信任的事情，而且他们的动机与我的一致。我给他们钱，他们给我很好的搜索结果。

>人们变得越来越愤世嫉俗，这是理所当然的。他们这样对社会有好处。

我不认为这是好事。民主是建立在信任之上的。没有信任，他们就会死亡，正如我们现在在美国可以清楚地看到的那样，人们彼此如此不信任，以至于开始破坏维系社会本身的结构。

children.author: bilbo0s
children.textRawHtml:
The entire point of the Constitution was to not trust each other. We wouldn't even need checks or balances if trust were a reasonable governing philosophy. Nor a Bill of Rights, nor any explicit admonition against slavery.

The reason we need these and many more is that we don't trust each other. And it's wise that we don't.

This doesn't mean trust is bad, only that the founders, rightfully, knew that it leads to complacency.

What's landed us where we are is not a lack of trust. (We've lacked trust from day one, that's how we got the Constitution in the first place). What's landed us here is complacency.
children.textZhHtml:
宪法的全部要点是不相互信任。如果信任是一种合理的治理理念，我们甚至不需要制衡。也没有权利法案，也没有任何反对奴隶制的明确警告。

我们需要这些以及更多的原因是我们彼此不信任。我们不这样做是明智的。

这并不意味着信任不好，只是创始人理所当然地知道信任会导致自满。

我们之所以走到今天这一步，并不是因为缺乏信任。 （我们从第一天起就缺乏信任，这就是我们最初制定宪法的方式）。让我们来到这里的是自满。

children.author: InsideOutSanta
children.textRawHtml: It's distrust - in foreigners, in each other - that got extremists elected, and it's distrust - in the media, in the institutions - that gives them unchecked power to destroy the country.
children.textZhHtml: 正是对外国人、彼此之间的不信任让极端分子当选，正是对媒体、机构的不信任赋予了他们摧毁国家的不受限制的权力。

children.author: bilbo0s
children.textRawHtml:
None of which would have happened if we had not gotten so complacent about defending our democracy through vigorous enforcement of the Constitution, and voting in a fashion more informed by wisdom than emotion.

At the root of all you lament is not lack of trust, it is complacency. Decades long complacency.

Lack of trust is what our republic was founded on, and the entire reason it was structured the way it was. Our republic is specifically designed to function in a trust-free environment.

But no republic, nor any other organizational construct, no matter how well designed, will survive complacency.
children.textZhHtml:
如果我们没有如此自满地通过大力执行宪法来捍卫我们的民主，并以智慧而非情感的方式进行投票，那么这一切都不会发生。

您所哀叹的根源不是缺乏信任，而是自满。几十年来的自满情绪。

缺乏信任是我们共和国的基础，也是它如此构建的全部原因。我们的共和国是专门设计在无信任环境中运作的。

但是，任何共和国或任何其他组织结构，无论设计得多么好，都无法自满。

comments.author: astrobiased
comments.textRawHtml:
The post hits it spot on with unequal access to the models in terms of security. I'm developing OSS where security is important for the user ... but the frontier models like GPT 5.6 and Fable flake out and state that I cannot get the info/access.

This is extremely lopsided I'll have to resort to GLM 5.2/K3 to ensure that those security issues (hopefully) are resolved properly.

For OSS, this is one of the most counterintuitive experiences I have ever had. More than ever I'm convinced that open weight and open pipelines models are 100% critical for progress on the AI and societal fronts.
comments.textZhHtml:
这篇文章切中要害，在安全性方面对模型的访问不平等。我正在开发 OSS，其中安全性对用户来说很重要......但是像 GPT 5.6 和 Fable 这样的前沿模型会崩溃并声明我无法获取信息/访问权限。

这是极其不平衡的，我必须诉诸 GLM 5.2/K3 来确保这些安全问题（希望）得到正确解决。

对于 OSS，这是我经历过的最违反直觉的体验之一。我比以往任何时候都更加相信开放权重和开放管道模型对于人工智能和社会前沿的进步 100% 至关重要。

children.author: bigyabai
children.textRawHtml:
Part of me wonders if the US Government is muzzling Anthropic and OpenAI so they can stockpile NOBUS exploits: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NOBUS

There would be a decently large incentive to restrict these models if they could be used to patch (or discover) dangerous payloads. In larger projects like Windows or Chrome, there might still be dozens of unpatched exploits that are too subtle to catch with smaller models.
children.textZhHtml:
我部分在想，美国政府是不是在封杀Anthropic和OpenAI的嘴，以便他们囤积NOBUS漏洞利用： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NOBUS

如果这些模型能用来修补（或发现）危险的有效载荷，那么限制这些模型就会有相当大的动机。在像Windows或Chrome这样的大型项目中，可能仍然有数十个未修补的漏洞，这些漏洞太微妙，较小的模型无法捕捉到。

children.author: wanick
children.textRawHtml: Hanlon's razor. It's not the NSA muzzling anyone, it's lawyers terrified of a bad headline. Same outcome, much dumber reason.
children.textZhHtml: 汉隆剃须刀。并不是美国国家安全局压制了任何人，而是律师们害怕糟糕的标题。同样的结果，愚蠢得多的理由。

children.textZhHtml: 这可能有多种原因。不要忘记，恶意行为者喜欢躲在 Hanlon 的后面。

NOBUS exploits have rarely been a driving interest for elected officials. Trade restrictions and reciprocity are far more salient and legible. Most elected officials are only barely aware of what NOBUS exploits even mean.

Even during the pre-Snowden heyday of US cyber supremacy, these capabilities were barely part of the thought process of White House officials.

NOBUS 漏洞很少引起民选官员的兴趣。贸易限制和互惠更加明显和清晰。大多数民选官员几乎不知道 NOBUS 漏洞的含义。

children.author: bigyabai
children.textRawHtml:
Conversely, the United States is now embroiled deeper in asymmetric warfare than ever before. US-based systems are being exploited by Chinese efforts like Salt Typhoon and raising questions about reciprocal attacks. Other targets of US soft-power like Iran (Stuxnet victim) are escalating their hacking efforts and using Chinese technology to stifle American command and control.

I can believe that NOBUS and other backdoors were ignored for a long time, but I have a hard time believing that it's being ignored by the current administration.
children.textZhHtml:
相反，美国现在比以往任何时候都更深入地卷入不对称战争。美国的系统正在被中国的“盐台风”等攻击所利用，并引发了相互攻击的问题。美国软实力的其他目标，例如伊朗（Stuxnet 受害者）正在升级其黑客攻击，并利用中国技术来扼杀美国的指挥和控制。

我可以相信 NOBUS 和其他后门被忽视很长一段时间，但我很难相信它被现任政府忽视。

>Agree, China believes in Climate change and are at least taking steps to address it.

China brought 80GW of new coal power online last year. The US added 0, and plans to add 3GW next year (we all know why).

China doesn't care about climate change, they care about energy independence, and conveniently have very little natural fossil fuels besides coal. Which they heavily mine and utilize.

children.author: DanielHB
children.textRawHtml:
The new coal plants are not planned to run a full capacity (or at all) they are meant for backup in case of environmental disaster and geopolitics (war, sanctions, tariffs, etc). So the actual amount of coal being burned is still going down.

Although it is highly suspicious why they are building so much unused capacity, it is as if they expect something to happen soon.
children.textZhHtml:
新燃煤电厂并未计划满负荷运行（或根本不运行），它们的目的是在发生环境灾难和地缘政治（战争、制裁、关税等）时作为备用。因此，实际燃烧的煤炭量仍在下降。

尽管人们非常怀疑他们为何要建设如此多的闲置产能，但他们似乎预计很快就会发生什么事情。

children.author: speedstyle
children.textRawHtml:
They commissioned 78 GW of new capacity in 2025, but actual coal-fired generation fell slightly (by 90 TWh, ie 10 GW). It might rise again, but it's expected to peak by 2027–28. And of course they added 440 GW of renewable capacity, 64% of global installations.

I certainly agree there's other motives (renewables even net them significant trade, like EVs) but it's not as though 'caring about' climate impacts requires selfless virtue, they stand to lose $trillions/year like everyone else.
children.textZhHtml:
他们在2025年投产了78吉瓦的新增产能，但实际燃煤发电量略有下降（减少了90太瓦时，即10吉瓦）。它可能会再次上升，但预计将在 2027-28 年达到顶峰。当然，他们增加了 440 吉瓦的可再生能源装机容量，占全球装机容量的 64%。

我当然同意还有其他动机（可再生能源甚至为他们带来了重要的贸易，例如电动汽车），但这并不意味着“关心”气候影响需要无私的美德，他们会像其他人一样每年损失数万亿美元。

children.author: Kon5ole
children.textRawHtml:
Good writeup, and certainly food for thought.

I wonder if this key point actually holds though:

>The skills to do so, however, are a function of someone who has experienced the friction and challenges over time that culminate in "good taste".

It's certainly sometimes true, but I don't think it's a general rule. Sometimes friction is just friction and sometimes you spend 1000 hours learning something that disappears and becomes obsolete or at least irrelevant to the goal.

Programmers used to need to know the instruction set of the CPU, assembly language and so on. Some still do but for most developers today that's not useful. Everything you know about 6800 assembly will not make your note-taking app any better.

I think we are in a state where AI tools so easily mimic what we used to do by hand that we think the friction is gone, but that's because we haven't raised the bar yet. One day we'll look at the Fable one-shot that's better than anything we ever made by hand ourselves and say "It could be even better".

And then the friction is back, just on a new level.
children.textZhHtml:
写得很好，当然值得深思。

我想知道这个关键点是否真的成立：

>然而，这样做的技能是一个经历过长期摩擦和挑战并最终达到“良好品味”的人的函数。

这有时确实是正确的，但我不认为这是一条普遍规则。有时摩擦只是摩擦，有时你花了 1000 小时学习一些东西，但这些东西消失了，变得过时了，或者至少与目标无关。

程序员过去需要了解 CPU 的指令集、汇编语言等。有些仍然这样做，但对于今天的大多数开发人员来说，这没有用。您所了解的关于 6800 组装的所有知识都不会让您的笔记应用程序变得更好。

我认为我们正处于这样一种状态：人工智能工具可以轻松模仿我们过去用手做的事情，我们认为摩擦已经消失，但那是因为我们还没有提高标准。有一天，我们会看到比我们自己亲手制作的任何作品都更好的《神鬼寓言》，并说“它可能会更好”。

然后摩擦又回来了，只是达到了一个新的水平。

children.textZhHtml:
> 您所了解的关于 6800 组装的一切并不会让您的笔记应用程序变得更好。

可以的。它可以使一个简单的笔记应用程序不占用千兆字节的内存，并且每次点击都会出现明显的延迟。大多数人当然不会打扰，因为简单的笔记应用程序不值得付出努力。可以做得更好，但通常不切实际。

children.author: rkuodys
children.textRawHtml: I am not sure if I'm ignorant or have too much self esteem, but in cases where domain is not my expertise I try to cross challenge using different models to get less-biased and hopefully more correct insights.
children.textZhHtml: 我不确定自己是否无知或太自尊，但在领域不是我的专业知识的情况下，我会尝试使用不同的模型来应对挑战，以获得更少偏见并希望获得更正确的见解。

children.author: beng-nl
children.textRawHtml:
I’m not trying to be snarky, but.. I wouldn’t try to upgrade an answer from a stranger you can’t fully trust to an expert answer you can trust by asking more strangers who have all only read the same books and use the same system for summarizing the books.

Similarly, sometimes I have to manually validate the output of an ai tool, analyzed over a large text I can’t practically read and understand, and best way I’ve found is to ask the tool (or another ai) to ‘show your work,’ ie make it help me make the determination by showing places in the text I have to read to follow its reasoning. We can never trust another ai directly to assess the validity of another ai.
children.textZhHtml:
我并不是想刻薄，但是..我不会试图通过询问更多只读过相同书籍并使用相同系统来总结书籍的陌生人，将您不能完全信任的陌生人的答案升级为您可以信任的专家答案。

同样，有时我必须手动验证人工智能工具的输出，对我实际上无法阅读和理解的大文本进行分析，我发现的最好方法是要求该工具（或另一个人工智能）“展示你的作品”，即让它通过显示我必须阅读的文本中的位置来遵循其推理来帮助我做出决定。我们永远不能直接信任另一个人工智能来评估另一个人工智能的有效性。

children.author: gamerDude
children.textRawHtml:
This is some work that I want to see in education. How do you validate if something is true or not.

A lot of times that will take physical tests. Or in the case of math/logic, tests to validate each line or validated sources of previously proved theorems
children.textZhHtml:
这是我想在教育领域看到的一些作品。如何验证某件事是否属实。

很多时候需要进行物理测试。或者在数学/逻辑的情况下，进行测试以验证每一行或验证先前证明的定理的来源

children.author: satvikpendem
children.textRawHtml: The paradox is analogous to AI poisoning its own training data as more and more AI generated content is released on the Internet. Indeed, I see a hard terminus for both man and machine at some point.
children.textZhHtml: 这个悖论类似于随着越来越多的人工智能生成的内容在互联网上发布，人工智能毒害了自己的训练数据。事实上，我认为人类和机器在某个时刻都面临着一个艰难的终点站。

children.author: dnemmers
children.textRawHtml: Absolutely right. First gen models are trained on 'virgin' non-LLM output. Subsequent models are tainted by ingesting AI replies. Rinse and repeat, and all you end up with is AI copies of AI replies, and the noise will completely overtake the signal.
children.textZhHtml: 完全正确。第一代模型是在“原始”非 LLM 输出上进行训练的。随后的模型因摄取人工智能回复而受到污染。冲洗并重复，你最终得到的只是人工智能回复的人工智能副本，噪音将完全超过信号。

children.author: literalAardvark
children.textRawHtml:
I'd have been more worried about that had the models not gotten incredibly good over time.

But they did get good and this seems like a non-issue.
children.textZhHtml:
如果模型没有随着时间的推移变得非常好，我会更担心这一点。

但它们确实变得很好，这似乎不是问题。

stories.title: Passkeys were invented by engineers with zero understanding of consumer brain
stories.titleZh: 万能钥匙是由对消费者大脑零了解的工程师发明的

comments.author: gortok
comments.textRawHtml:
I do not know how to use a Passkey in a way that won’t impede how I log in to systems. I’ve been in tech for 26 years, and I understand the Public/private key behind what a Passkey is. Here’s what I don’t understand:

I access a website through at least four different devices (my iPad, iPhone, Windows Desktop computer, and MacBook Pro) and three different browsers on each device (Brave, Firefox, Safari) , and I use LastPass. If I accidentally set up a passkey on my phone (let’s say I use Safari one day instead of my go-to, Brave), can I still log in without that passkey on other devices? Is there a way to ensure that passkey can be used on other devices? Can I add another passkey on another device? How many passkeys can I set up for a particular site/app? I have at least 6 different combination of browser/devices in use.

I don’t want to use Passkeys because I don’t the answers to those questions, and I don’t know whether each website/app that has set up Passkeys has decided the answers to those questions in the same way as the others. For now, I’m going to stick with LastPass and use Passwords; because no matter whether I lose my device or not or whether I’m on my own devices or not, I can be sure I’ll be able to get into a site/app.

Edit: One final consideration, my spouse and I share user/name passwords for some things (notably Pandora and our Amazon Prime account) since they don’t handle things like family logins well; how do both my wife and I use amazon or Pandora with passkeys? Do we each set up passkeys? How do I get her Pass if that’s not an option?
comments.textZhHtml:
我不知道如何以不妨碍我登录系统的方式使用密钥。我在科技领域工作了 26 年，我了解密钥背后的公钥/私钥。以下是我不明白的地方：

我通过至少四种不同的设备（我的 iPad、iPhone、Windows 台式电脑和 MacBook Pro）以及每台设备上的三种不同的浏览器（Brave、Firefox、Safari）访问网站，并且我使用 LastPass。 如果我不小心在手机上设置了密码（假设有一天我使用 Safari 而不是我常用的 Brave），我是否仍然可以在其他设备上没有该密码的情况下登录？有没有办法确保密钥可以在其他设备上使用？ 我可以在另一台设备上添加另一个密钥吗？我可以为特定网站/应用程序设置多少个密钥？我至少使用了 6 种不同的浏览器/设备组合。

我不想使用 Passkeys，因为我不知道这些问题的答案，而且我不知道每个设置了 Passkeys 的网站/应用程序是否都以与其他网站/应用程序相同的方式决定了这些问题的答案。 现在，我将坚持使用 LastPass 并使用密码；因为无论我是否丢失设备，或者是否使用自己的设备，我都可以确定我能够进入网站/应用程序。

编辑：最后一个考虑因素是，我和我的配偶共享某些内容的用户/名称密码（特别是 Pandora 和我们的 Amazon Prime 帐户），因为他们不能很好地处理家庭登录等问题；我和我的妻子如何通过密钥使用亚马逊或潘多拉？我们每个人都设置密钥吗？如果这不是一个选择，我如何获得她的通行证？

children.author: thecombjelly
children.textRawHtml:
A potentially good idea got corrupted by vendors, password managers, browsers, etc trying to assert control. I'm also an engineer and I find the UI around passkeys entirely unclear, but it doesn't have to be that way. It seems like everyone wants to be _the_ password manager for all your passkeys. They don't want to make it easy to understand that is what they are doing though, they just happily offer to "handle it for you".

My non-technical friends are extremely confused by passkeys and if they should use them and how to use them and I honestly don't have very good answers. It is a mess. I don't believe an inherit mess, but one created by the companies and projects trying to take advantage of the new system.
children.textZhHtml:
一个潜在的好主意被试图主张控制权的供应商、密码管理器、浏览器等破坏了。我也是一名工程师，我发现密钥周围的用户界面完全不清楚，但它不一定是这样的。似乎每个人都想成为所有万能钥匙的密码管理器。他们不想让人们容易理解他们正在做的事情，他们只是很高兴地提出“为你处理它”。

我的非技术朋友对万能钥匙非常困惑，他们是否应该使用它们以及如何使用它们，老实说我没有很好的答案。真是一团糟。我不相信继承的混乱，而是由试图利用新系统的公司和项目造成的混乱。

children.author: thewebguyd
children.textRawHtml:
> It seems like everyone wants to be _the_ password manager for all your passkeys.

Which defeats part of the point of passkeys in the first place in that they are supposed to be device-bound, the private key held in the TPM or secure enclave or whatever other security chip, mathematically non-exportable. Storing all your private keys in a cloud vault still leaves you exposed to potential credential theft if your vault gets compromised.

Every device is supposed to have its own unique private key, stored in TPM, released only when passing the user challenge (biometrics or pin, or a yubikey).
children.textZhHtml:
> 似乎每个人都想成为所有万能钥匙的密码管理器。

这首先就违背了万能钥匙的部分要点，因为它们应该是设备绑定的，私钥保存在 TPM 或安全飞地或任何其他安全芯片中，从数学上讲是不可导出的。如果您的保管库受到威胁，将所有私钥存储在云保管库中仍然会让您面临潜在的凭据盗窃风险。

每个设备都应该有自己唯一的私钥，存储在 TPM 中，仅在通过用户质询（生物识别或 PIN 码或 yubikey）时释放。

children.author: dotancohen
children.textRawHtml:
> Every device is supposed to have its own unique private key, stored in TPM, released only when passing the user challenge (biometrics or pin, or a yubikey).
I have just shy of 2000 site credentials in Keepass. Let's assume that they were all Passkeys.

1) When I buy a new device, how do I create 2000 new Passkeys for that device?

2) Can I still do that if I don't have access to the old device? Maybe it was destroyed, stolen, or lost.

3) How about if the new device is from a different vendor than the original device? E.g. switching from Apple to Android?

我在 Keepass 中只有不到 2000 个站点凭据。假设它们都是密钥。

1) 当我购买新设备时，如何为该设备创建 2000 个新密钥？

2) 如果我无法访问旧设备，我还能这样做吗？也许它被毁坏、被盗或丢失。

3) 如果新设备来自与原始设备不同的供应商怎么办？例如。从苹果切换到安卓？

children.author: crote
children.textRawHtml:
The original U2F tokens didn't suffer from this problem that much. It contained one secret, it relied on the website to store and return an opaque website-and-account-specific blob, which was decrypted by the token and used to sign the login request. It would be reasonably easy to expand this in a way where the server also stores a backup credential for use by your second/third/whatever hardware token, encrypted with an asymmetric key during the enrollment of the primary token.

But this went out of the window when some genius decided that usernames were too complicated, so Passkeys had to be discoverable, which means they have to be fully stored token-side. Which of course has the nice side benefit of essentially killing hardware tokens and forcing people into using their Android/iOS/Windows device for it.
children.textZhHtml:
原始 U2F 代币并没有受到太多这个问题的困扰。它包含一个秘密，它依赖网站来存储和返回不透明的网站和帐户特定的 blob，该 blob 由令牌解密并用于签署登录请求。通过服务器还存储备份凭证以供第二个/第三个/任何硬件令牌使用的备份凭据，在主令牌注册期间使用非对称密钥进行加密，这将相当容易进行扩展。

但是，当一些天才认为用户名太复杂时，这种情况就被排除在外了，因此密码必须是可发现的，这意味着它们必须完全存储在令牌端。当然，这还有一个很好的副作用，那就是基本上消除了硬件令牌并迫使人们使用他们的 Android/iOS/Windows 设备。

children.author: omnifischer
children.textRawHtml: This. I would have loved if this original u2f existed. Google has even removed this from their accounts.
children.textZhHtml: 这个。如果这个原始的 u2f 存在的话我会很高兴。谷歌甚至已将其从他们的帐户中删除。

children.author: chimeracoder
children.textRawHtml:
> But this went out of the window when some genius decided that usernames were too complicated, so Passkeys had to be discoverable, which means they have to be fully stored token-side. Which of course has the nice side benefit of essentially killing hardware tokens and forcing people into using their Android/iOS/Windows device for it.

This part of your comment is incorrect. Discoverable Webauthn absolutely can be used with hardware tokens like Yubikeys.
children.textZhHtml:
> 但是，当一些天才认为用户名太复杂，因此密钥必须是可发现的，这意味着它们必须完全存储在令牌端时，这就被排除在外了。当然，这有一个很好的附带好处，即基本上消除了硬件令牌并迫使人们使用他们的 Android/iOS/Windows 设备。

您的这部分评论是不正确的。可发现的 Webauthn 绝对可以与 Yubikeys 等硬件令牌一起使用。

children.author: Itoldmyselfso
children.textRawHtml:
Passkey comprises of public key that the website you created it on holds, and the private key you store on your pw manager or in the TPM/secure element. As long as you can copy the private key to the new device, you don't need to recreate the whole passkey. In your case, you would just need access to a backup of the Keepass database in case you lose the device.

The biggest point of confusion in my opinion comes from Windows especially having lacked a way (and kind of still does) to save the private key of a passkey to your password manager, defaulting to saving it to Windows Hello, which saves the private key to your PC's TPM. In this scenario you can no longer easily copy the private key to other devices, and if you lose that Windows PC, you also lose the private key and the whole passkey as a result.
children.textZhHtml:
密码由您创建的网站持有的公钥以及您存储在密码管理器或 TPM/安全元件中的私钥组成。只要您可以将私钥复制到新设备，就无需重新创建整个密钥。就您而言，您只需要访问 Keepass 数据库的备份，以防丢失

在我看来，最大的困惑来自于 Windows，尤其是它缺乏一种方法（现在仍然如此）将密钥的私钥保存到密码管理器中，默认将其保存到 Windows Hello，后者将私钥保存到 PC 的 TPM 中。在这种情况下，您无法再轻松地将私钥复制到其他设备，并且如果您丢失了该 Windows PC，您也会因此丢失私钥和整个密钥。

children.author: pseudalopex
children.textRawHtml: People who say users should not be able to export keys are not confused. They believe users should not be able to export keys.
children.textZhHtml: 那些说用户不应该能够导出密钥的人并不感到困惑。他们认为用户不应该能够导出密钥。

children.author: Itoldmyselfso
children.textRawHtml: I was referring to confusion experienced by people new to the passkeys creating passkeys for the first time ("Where am I saving this to?", "How do I store this in my password manager?").
children.textZhHtml: 我指的是第一次创建密钥的新手所经历的困惑（“我将其保存到哪里？”，“如何将其存储在我的密码管理器中？”）。

children.author: ivlad
children.textRawHtml:
You don’t. You don’t store anything at all.

On registration, a keypair is generated, then the private key is encrypted with the long-term key burned into your security key fob or hardware. The encrypted blob is sent to the server and stored there.

On authentication, after you enter your login, the server sends the encrypted blob and your security key tries to decrypt it with the long-term key it has. If it succeeds, it then request a challenge from the servers, signs it along with the server name and timestamp and sends back to the server. Server validates the signature and if it’s good, log you in.

Expanded: As long you as the user has the security key fob, you can login. You should have 2.
children.textZhHtml:
你不知道。您根本不存储任何内容。

注册时，会生成密钥对，然后使用刻录到安全密钥卡或硬件中的长期密钥对私钥进行加密。加密的 blob 被发送到服务器并存储在那里。

在进行身份验证时，在您输入登录名后，服务器会发送加密的 blob，并且您的安全密钥会尝试使用其拥有的长期密钥对其进行解密。如果成功，它会向服务器请求质询，将其与服务器名称和时间戳一起签名，然后发送回服务器。服务器验证签名，如果签名正确，则登录。

扩展：只要您作为用户拥有安全密钥，就可以登录。您应该有 2 个。

children.author: doodlesdev
children.textRawHtml:
> You don’t. You don’t store anything at all.

Yes, you do.

Whenever a website offers to create a passkey, it could end up in any of these:

- Samsung's Password Manager (if using a Samsung phone)

- Apple's Keychain (if using an iPhone)

- Google Password Manager

- Your operating system's keychain

- A bespoke password manager (e.g., Bitwarden or LastPass)

- Your hardware key

Most users do not have a security key fob. Instead, the proposal being mostly pushed is the idea that users can store keys on their own smartphones, making use of the modern TPM and chip security. Most of the discussion here revolves around that idea: "What if I lose my phone? What if I switch phones?" and that's why the problems seem so obvious to you.

I absolutely agree the best solution is to use hardware keys, but I'll admit it's cumbersome if I need them for hundreds of accounts (which I do have), having to register both for every website and praying that I never lose both at the same time in the case there's no viable recovery flow for some of the accounts. Also, most of these hardware keys are limited to 25 or 100 resident keys, which again makes them unable to substitute passwords. Observe the usage of the term resident keys: passkeys do rely on the private key being stored on the hardware key, as that allows discovery.
children.textZhHtml:
> 你不知道。您根本不存储任何内容。

是的，您会这样做。

每当网站提供创建密钥时，它最终可能会出现在以下任何一个中：

- 三星的密码管理器（如果使用三星手机）

- Apple 的钥匙串（如果使用 iPhone）

- Google 密码管理器

- 您操作系统的钥匙串

- 定制的密码管理器（例如 Bitwarden 或 LastPass）

- 您的硬件密钥

大多数用户没有安全密钥卡。相反，主要推动的提案是用户可以利用现代 TPM 和芯片安全性将密钥存储在自己的智能手机上。这里的大部分讨论都围绕这个想法：“如果我丢了手机怎么办？如果我换手机怎么办？”这就是为什么问题对你来说如此明显。

我绝对同意最好的解决方案是使用硬件密钥，但我承认，如果我需要为数百个帐户（我确实有）使用它们，这会很麻烦，必须为每个网站注册这两个帐户，并祈祷我永远不会同时丢失这两个帐户，以防某些帐户没有可行的恢复流程。此外，大多数这些硬件密钥仅限于 25 或 100 个常驻密钥，这又使得它们无法替换密码。观察术语“驻留密钥”的用法：万能密钥确实依赖于存储在硬件密钥上的私钥，因为这允许发现。

children.author: ivlad
children.textRawHtml:
I was talking about the non-resident FIDO keys. “Passkey” term is meaningless unfortunately because FIDO Alliance did not define it initially, it was a marketing term invented by Apple and then re-introduced (or shoved down the throat) by the FIDO alliance.

In non-resident keys scenario you don’t store anything and from what I see there is no security downside of using non-resident keys.

Loosing both (or multiple) security keys is like loosing all your car or home keys. Very inconvenient, agreed.

Anyway, I think we can agree that FIDO authentication protocol implementation is a mess. Apple and Google made it messy because they wanted to lock down users to their platforms and then password managers followed. As a result, the current implementation is not more secure than “login with Apple” or “login with Google”.
children.textZhHtml:
我正在谈论非驻留 FIDO 密钥。不幸的是，“密钥”一词毫无意义，因为 FIDO 联盟最初并未对其进行定义，它是 Apple 发明的营销术语，然后由 FIDO 联盟重新引入（或强行强行推行）。

在非居民密钥场景中，您不会存储任何内容，从我看来，使用非居民密钥不会带来安全问题。

丢失两个（或多个）安全密钥就像丢失所有汽车或家庭钥匙一样。非常不方便，同意。

无论如何，我想我们可以同意FIDO认证协议的实现是一团糟。苹果和谷歌让事情变得混乱，因为他们想将用户锁定在他们的平台上，然后密码管理器也随之而来。因此，当前的实现并不比“使用 Apple 登录”或“使用 Google 登录”更安全。

children.author: pseudalopex
children.textRawHtml: dotancohen would not be allowed to store Passkeys private keys in a Keepass database in the case thewebguyd specified. Relying parties would block password managers which allowed this.
children.textZhHtml: 在 thewebguyd 指定的情况下，不允许 dotancohen 将 Passkeys 私钥存储在 Keepass 数据库中。依赖方会阻止允许这样做的密码管理器。

children.author: ilchalpenl
children.textRawHtml:
> Let's assume that they were all Passkeys.

children.author: ijidak
children.textRawHtml:
Why wouldn't the hyperscalers run these open models since they're much better than OpenAI and Anthropic at operating compute at scale?

I think the reason OpenAI and Anthropic stay ahead in revenues right now is because the models are improving too quickly to reliably compete with them on cost.

But, once model performance reaches a plateau -- they have to at some point, though perhaps years away -- that's when ability to operate compute infrastructure at scale becomes the secret sauce.

The big AI labs are likely safe until models stop improving fast enough to protect them from competition on cost.

This similar pattern has repeated in most technical booms prior to this.

When hard drive technology was improving fast enough that old hard drives were quickly obsolete, IBM could maintain good margins making hard drives. But once hard drives got good enough and advances were slow enough that innovation was not the only factor considered by drive purchasers, commodity hard drives started to take over and IBM had to exit those businesses.

The same is likely to happen once model improvement slows.
children.textZhHtml:
为什么超大规模企业不运行这些开放模型，因为它们在大规模运行计算方面比 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 好得多？

我认为 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 目前在收入方面保持领先的原因是这些模型改进得太快，无法在成本上与他们进行可靠的竞争。

但是，一旦模型性能达到稳定水平 - 他们必须在某个时候，尽管可能需要几年时间 - 那时就到了大规模运行计算基础设施的能力成为秘密武器。

大型人工智能实验室可能是安全的，直到模型停止快速改进以保护它们免受成本竞争的影响。

这种类似的模式在此前的大多数技术繁荣中都曾重复过。

当硬盘技术进步得足够快，旧硬盘很快就会被淘汰时，IBM 可以在硬盘制造方面保持良好的利润。但是，一旦硬盘驱动器变得足够好并且进步足够缓慢，创新不再是驱动器购买者考虑的唯一因素，商品硬盘开始占据主导地位，IBM 不得不退出这些业务。

一旦模型改进放缓，同样的情况也可能发生。

children.author: senko
children.textRawHtml:
Having tested K3, Qwen 3.8 max preview, Fable and Sol for the past few days, I partially agree. Don’t trust the benchmarks, and the Chinese models really are slow and token-inefficient.

However they do seem very close to SOTA : I’d say roughly equal to previous gen (Opus 4.8, GPT 5.5). It’s yet another silly benchmark, but compare them here: https://senko.net/vibecode-bench/

I also had K3, Qwen3.8 and Fable (using Kimi Code, Qwen Code and Claude Code harnesses respectively, and the official APIs) create a simple but far from trivial web app (zero shot, from a detailed spec). In user testing all three results looked/behaved more or less the same. I had Sol (via codex) do code reviews on all three and it concluded all three were solid, with some room for improvement. Fable was slightly ahead of the pack.

In my own work I still prefer Opus 4.8 (until Antrophic come to their senses and allow 100% Fable usage on Max plans) and Sol, but if I had to find an alternative, I could live with both K3 and Qwen3.8 just fine.

我还使用了 K3、Qwen3.8 和 Fable（分别使用 Kimi Code、Qwen Code 和 Claude Code 工具以及官方 API）创建了一个简单但远非平凡的 Web 应用程序（零镜头，来自详细规范）。在用户测试中，所有三个结果看起来/行为或多或少相同。我让 Sol（通过 codex）对这三个项目进行了代码审查，得出的结论是这三个项目都很可靠，还有一些改进的空间。 Fable 稍稍领先。

在我自己的工作中，我仍然更喜欢 Opus 4.8（直到 Antropicic 醒悟并允许 Max 计划 100% 使用 Fable）和 Sol，但如果我必须找到替代方案，我可以同时使用 K3 和 Qwen3.8。

children.author: senko

For the web app task I mentioned:

* Qwen 3.8 Max: 18020k input (17836k cached), 114k output - cost $6.3

* Fable: ~14m input (all cached??), 196k output - cost $30

Correction on my earlier post, Kimi was through Pi, not Kimi Code. For Qwen I used Qwen Code and for Fable I used Claude Code.

Not sure wtf is going on with the Fable stats (a lot tokens, virtuall all of them were cached - I guess heavy system prompt?) but both claude code stats and ccusage tool output match.
children.textZhHtml:
对于我提到的网络应用程序任务：

* Kimi K3：9532k 输入（9172k 缓存）、114k 输出 - 成本 5.5 美元

* Qwen 3.8 Max：18020k 输入（17836k 缓存）、114k 输出 - 成本 6.3 美元

* Fable：~14m输入（全部缓存？？），196k 输出 - 成本 30 美元

更正我之前的帖子，Kimi 是通过 Pi，而不是 Kimi 代码。对于 Qwen，我使用 Qwen Code，对于 Fable，我使用 Claude Code。

不确定 Fable 统计数据是否正常（很多标记，几乎所有标记都被缓存 - 我猜系统提示很重？），但是 claude 代码统计数据和 ccusage 工具输出都匹配。

children.author: makingstuffs
children.textRawHtml: Would a fairer test not be to use the same harness for all three? I’d suspect the harness to massively affect token use and optimisation
children.textZhHtml: 更公平的测试不是为所有三个使用相同的安全带吗？我怀疑该工具会极大地影响代币的使用和优化

children.author: JaRail
children.textRawHtml: Yes and no. It would make the test fair but it would also mean losing any optimizations the vendors have made specifically for their model. They're all trained differently so they should be used differently to get maximum benefit. It makes sense to use each vendor's harness to get the most of their models, and compare the results that way. Additionally, it's not easy to use all the models in all the harnesses. Anthropic's subscription only covers claude code for example.
children.textZhHtml: 是也不是。这将使测试公平，但也意味着失去供应商专门为其模型所做的任何优化。他们都接受过不同的训练，因此应该以不同的方式使用他们以获得最大的利益。使用每个供应商的工具来充分利用他们的模型并以这种方式比较结果是有意义的。此外，在所有线束中使用所有型号并不容易。例如，Anthropic 的订阅仅涵盖克劳德代码。

children.author: makingstuffs
children.textRawHtml:
I don’t know that the methodology of the experiment is testing what it intends to test. With the current method we’re essentially testing each AI lab’s ability to efficiently extract data from their version of a transformer model.

To objectively test all models the harness would need to be the same and ideally independent. Failing that, all three models should be tested in all three harnesses and the output verified on a model x harness level and on an overall aggregated model x all harnesses level.
children.textZhHtml:
我不知道实验的方法论是在测试它想要测试的东西。使用当前的方法，我们实质上是在测试每个人工智能实验室从其变压器模型版本中有效提取数据的能力。

为了客观地测试所有模型，线束需要相同并且理想情况下是独立的。如果做不到这一点，则应在所有三个线束中测试所有三个模型，并在模型 x 线束级别和总体聚合模型 x 所有线束级别上验证输出。

children.author: maxignol
children.textRawHtml:
> I’d suspect the harness to massively affect token use and optimisation

Yes they do according to databricks -> https://www.databricks.com/blog/benchmarking-coding-agents-d...

That's why I find comparing models on benchmarks only gives the tendency. We should be comparing model x harness to have accurate metrics.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我怀疑该工具会极大地影响令牌的使用和优化

是的，根据 databricks -> https://www.databricks.com/blog/benchmarking-coding-agents-d...

这就是为什么我发现在基准上比较模型只会给出趋势。我们应该比较模型 x 线束以获得准确的指标。

children.author: makingstuffs
children.textRawHtml:
Agreed. An experiment is only as accurate as the methodology is sound.

Testing each lab’s model in its own harness just tells us how well the lab has performed. For the model’s performance we need a control and the only way to get the control is to either test all models in all harnesses or all models in the same harness
children.textZhHtml:
同意。实验的准确性取决于方法的合理性。

使用每个实验室自己的工具测试模型只能告诉我们实验室的表现如何。对于模型的性能，我们需要一个控件，获得控件的唯一方法是测试所有线束中的所有模型或同一线束中的所有模型

stories.title: Judge approves $1.5B Anthropic settlement for pirated books used to train Claude
stories.titleZh: 法官批准用于训练克劳德的盗版书籍的 $1.5B 人为和解

comments.author: sashank_1509
comments.textRawHtml: A one time payment like 1.5B doesn’t do anything. There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas. That is probably the correct way to legislate this. If anything a human does can instantly be copied by an LLM, and then sent to all its subscribers, things need to change
comments.textZhHtml: 像 1.5B 这样的一次性付款没有任何作用。需要根据人工智能是否反省现有想法来支付特许权使用费。这可能是立法的正确方式。如果法学硕士可以立即复制人类所做的任何事情，然后发送给其所有订阅者，那么情况就需要改变

children.author: phire
children.textRawHtml:
This settlement has basically nothing to do with LLMs.

At least not as far as the courts are concerned. Alsup ruled [0] that feeding a book into an LLM is transformative and counts as fair use. Especially when they purchased a physical copy of the book, scanned it, and destroyed the original.

But if I'm reading the ruling correctly, Anthropic might have been fine even with feeding pirated books into their LLM (as long as they planned to eventually deleted them afterwards)

Where Anthropic ran into problems is that they put all their pirated books into a big central library (file on a server), and planned to keep those copies forever. Including copies they never actually fed into the LLM (a point that seriously worked against them).

Alsup ruled this central library of pirated books was copyright infringement. And it's this "pirated central library" that Anthropic are now paying a a 1.5B settlement for, nothing else.

The fact that the pirated books were also used to train LLMs is legally irrelevant. Though... I suspect a non AI company could have negotiated a significantly smaller settlement.

[0] https://copyrightalliance.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Bar...
children.textZhHtml:
这项和解基本上与 LLM 无关。

至少就法院而言并非如此。 Alsup 裁定[0]将一本书纳入 LLM 具有变革性，并且算作合理使用。尤其是当他们购买了这本书的实体副本，扫描它并销毁了原件时。

但是，如果我正确地阅读了裁决，即使将盗版书籍送入他们的法学硕士，Anthropic 也可能没问题（只要他们计划最终删除它们）

Anthropic 遇到的问题是，他们将所有盗版书籍放入一个大型中央图书馆（服务器上的文件），并计划永远保留这些副本。包括他们从未真正输入法学硕士的副本（这一点对他们严重不利）。

阿尔苏普裁定这个中央图书馆的盗版书籍侵犯了版权。 Anthropic 现在支付 1.5B 和解金的正是这个“盗版中央图书馆”，仅此而已。

盗版书籍也被用来培训法学硕士的事实在法律上是无关紧要的。不过……我怀疑一家非人工智能公司本可以通过谈判达成小得多的和解协议。

[0] https://copyrightalliance.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Bar...

children.author: smolder
children.textRawHtml: As far as I'm concerned, the courts are wrong, and training on ill gotten copyrighted material is not fair use. Given the clear value of highly trained LLMs, the investment they have taken on, and the amount of disruption to the existing economy they stand to make, in a just world, the people who created the training data deserve some level of compensation. I think, in the US, they are very afraid of falling behind China, who doesn't give a shit about intellectual property, but that doesn't mean we aren't crossing an ethical boundary, acting like them.
children.textZhHtml: 就我而言，法院是错误的，对非法获取的受版权保护的材料进行培训并不属于合理使用。鉴于训练有素的法学硕士的明显价值、他们所进行的投资以及他们对现有经济造成的破坏程度，在一个公正的世界中，创建培训数据的人应该得到一定程度的补偿。我认为，在美国，他们非常害怕落后于中国，中国根本不关心知识产权，但这并不意味着我们没有像他们那样跨越道德界限。

children.author: dghlsakjg
children.textRawHtml:
> As far as I'm concerned, the courts are wrong, and training on ill gotten copyrighted material is not fair use.

It’s important to remember that a court’s job is to apply law to a situation. When a court gets something wrong it’s a misinterpretation of the law and will, by definition, be overturnable on appeal. I suspect that your objection isn’t that the court is wrong, it’s that the law is wrong.
children.textZhHtml:
> 就我而言，法院是错误的，对非法获得的受版权保护的材料进行培训并不属于合理使用。

重要的是要记住，法院的工作是将法律应用于特定情况。当法院犯错时，这是对法律的误解，根据定义，可以在上诉时推翻。我怀疑你的反对不是法院错了，而是法律错了。

children.author: smolder
children.textRawHtml: Yes, ultimately the problem is that the law is vague or inadequate. The courts have their definitions of fair use, which are their best efforts at interpreting the law, and I have mine, which is different.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，最终的问题是法律模糊或不充分。法院有他们对合理使用的定义，这是他们解释法律的最大努力，而我有我的定义，这是不同的。

children.author: themgt
children.textRawHtml:
William Roper: "So, now you give the Devil the benefit of law!"

Sir Thomas More: "Yes! What would you do? Cut a great road through the law to get after the Devil?"

William Roper: "Yes, I’d cut down every law in England to do that!"

Sir Thomas More: "Oh? And when the last law was down, and the Devil turned ’round on you, where would you hide, Roper, the laws all being flat? This country is planted thick with laws, from coast to coast, Man’s laws, not God’s! And if you cut them down, and you’re just the man to do it, do you really think you could stand upright in the winds that would blow then? Yes, I’d give the Devil benefit of law, for my own safety’s sake!"
children.textZhHtml:
威廉·罗珀：“那么，现在你给魔鬼以法律的好处！”

托马斯·莫尔爵士：“是的！你会做什么？通过法律开辟一条伟大的道路来追捕魔鬼？”

威廉·罗珀：“是的，我会废除英格兰的所有法律来做到这一点！”

托马斯爵士更多：“哦？当最后一条法律被废除，魔鬼转向你时，你会躲在哪里，罗珀，所有的法律都是平的？这个国家从东海岸到西海岸都布满了法律，是人的法律，而不是上帝的法律！如果你砍掉它们，而你就是这样做的人，你真的认为你可以在那时吹来的风中站起来吗？是的，为了我自己的安全，我会给魔鬼法律的好处。清酒！”

children.author: tetha
children.textRawHtml:
This is why the idea of being "Vogelfrei" or "lawless" was honestly a terrifying concept in the middle ages. They are neither bound by law, nor protected by law.

A lawless man can be struck down with force without persecution by law, because they are lawless.
children.textZhHtml:
这就是为什么“Vogelfrei”或“无法无天”的想法在中世纪确实是一个可怕的概念。他们不受法律约束，也不受法律保护。

无法无天的人可以被武力打倒而不受法律迫害，因为他们无法无天。

children.author: mycall
children.textRawHtml:

Doesn't this define modern day police force theory?
children.textZhHtml:
> 无法无天的人可以被武力打倒而不受法律迫害，因为他们无法无天。

children.author: tetha
children.textRawHtml:
I am not 100% familiar with this theory, but I'm fairly sure there is an important difference: The monopoly of violence current police forces are granted did not exist in this particular way.

If a lawless man robbed from a village, any of the villagers could do whatever they wanted to them in retribution. Maybe limited a bit by religion.
children.textZhHtml:
我不是100%熟悉这个理论，但我相当确定有一个重要的区别：当前警察部队所授予的暴力垄断并不以这种特殊方式存在。

如果一个不法分子从村庄里抢劫，任何村民都可以对他们做任何他们想做的事来报复。也许受到宗教的限制。

children.textZhHtml: 这句话很酷，但完全没用。你有强烈的意见，没有争论。

children.author: freejazz
children.textRawHtml: Sorry, I'm thinking of Kadrey, where the court rejected Anthropic's "training" argument and provided an explanation as to how author litigants should demonstrate market harm in order to succeed on a fair use analysis, a factor that Alsup did not effectively weigh.
children.textZhHtml: 抱歉，我想到了 Kadrey，法院驳回了 Anthropic 的“训练”论点，并解释了作者诉讼当事人应如何证明市场损害才能成功进行合理使用分析，而 Alsup 并未有效权衡这一因素。

children.author: keeda
children.textRawHtml:
I suspect the market harm angle is not going to work out either based on the one study I know of on the topic: https://www.nber.org/papers/w34777

> We document a tripling in the number of new books coming to market between late 2022 and late 2025 that mirrors the use of AI that we detect in new books. The effects of this influx on consumer welfare depend on the quality of the additional books. The average quality of new books has fallen with the LLM-induced influx, and books with detected AI are substantially worse than human-authored books, so that much of the new work is of little value to consumers. Still, the LLM influx has delivered some books in the middle range of the usage/quality distribution, and the LLM-era entry process delivered seven percent more consumer surplus from books than the pre-LLM process in 2025.

...

Moreover, the arrival of LLMs does not appear to have displaced activity by incumbent authors. Despite the controversy surrounding LLMs, their effect on book consumers – like other cost-reducing technological changes in the cultural industries – is positive. However, because the new books are mostly of low quality, the effects are modest

So not only are existing authors unharmed (because most of the new competition is slop) there is even a small improvement for consumers.
children.textZhHtml:
根据我所知道的关于该主题的一项研究，我怀疑市场危害角度也不会成立：https://www.nber.org/papers/w34777

> 我们记录了 2022 年底到 2025 年底期间上市的新书数量增加了两倍，这反映了我们在新书中检测到的人工智能的使用。这种涌入对消费者福利的影响取决于额外书籍的质量。随着法学硕士的涌入，新书的平均质量有所下降，而检测到人工智能的书籍比人类撰写的书籍要差得多，因此许多新书对消费者来说没有什么价值。尽管如此，法学硕士的涌入仍然带来了一些处于使用/质量分布中间范围的书籍，而且到 2025 年，法学硕士时代的入学流程比法学硕士之前的流程从书籍中获得的消费者剩余多了 7%。

...

children.author: Gormo
children.textRawHtml:
> While I do use old.reddit and genuinely think it's better UI-wise, in the general case users tend to be resistant to any UI change because it breaks their habits and muscle memory.

It's funny how often people say "it's just habit / muscle memory / etc." in response to these kinds of discussions, as though it were somehow a counter-argument, and not something that corroborates the argument against making sudden, drastic UI changes.

It's really weird how common it is for people to say "well, Y is only true because of X" in response to "Z doesn't work because of Y", and thinking they're somehow defeating the argument against Z. If you acknowledge that X exists and causes Y, and accept that Y isn't compatible with Z, then you are conceding that Z does not hold!

人们很常见地回答“Z 因为 Y 而不起作用”，并认为他们在某种程度上击败了反对 Z 的论点，这真的很奇怪。如果你承认 X 存在并导致 Y，并接受 Y与 Z 不兼容，那么你就承认 Z 不成立！

stories.title: Show HN: Bento - An entire PowerPoint in one HTML file (edit+view+data+collab)
stories.titleZh: 显示 HN：Bento - 一个 HTML 文件中的整个 PowerPoint（编辑+查看+数据+协作）

comments.author: starfallg
comments.textRawHtml:
Hi, I'm the creator of Bento. Just wanted to share a bit more about how I created it beyond what's in Github.

The file contains more or less two sections. There is a plain block of JSON near the top of the file which is the slide data. You can read, grep, or point a harness at it. The app itself is in a base64 blob that loads through a small shim which deflates in the browser with DecompressionStream, which keeps the package small and so that we don't need to fetch any external files at runtime. File System Access API is used for JSON writeback into the same file, which falls back to a plian download and all updates are ECDSA signed.

I started with reveal.js which served as a base, and incorporated GSAP/Flip to handle the animation. Then I added charting with echarts, but due to a number of issues with size and how the worked, I ended up re-implementing both.

What I'm most pleased about is how seamless the CRDT works. The blind relay is a small file that runs on Cloudflare Durable Objects, and all it sees are the encrypted data from the clients. Collab is off until we turn it on in the app (by starting a session by sharing by invite file), and then some sets of keys are generated. Access is handled by user key, and you can have read-only users, and also revoke live collab access by user as well.
comments.textZhHtml:
嗨，我是 Bento 的创建者。除了 Github 中的内容外，我只是想分享更多关于我如何创建它的信息。

该文件或多或少包含两个部分。文件顶部附近有一个 JSON 的普通块，它是幻灯片数据。您可以阅读、grep 或将线束指向它。应用程序本身位于一个 base64 blob 中，通过一个小垫片加载，该垫片在浏览器中使用 DecompressionStream 进行压缩，从而使包保持较小，因此我们不需要在运行时获取任何外部文件。文件系统访问 API 用于将 JSON 写回同一文件，该文件会回退到 plian 下载，并且所有更新均经过 ECDSA 签名。

我从用作基础的 Reveal.js 开始，并合并了 GSAP/Flip 来处理动画。然后我用 echarts 添加了图表，但由于尺寸和工作方式方面的一些问题，我最终重新实现了这两个图表。

我最满意的是 CRDT 的无缝工作方式。盲中继是一个在 Cloudflare Durable Objects 上运行的小文件，它看到的只是来自客户端的加密数据。协作处于关闭状态，直到我们在应用程序中将其打开（通过邀请文件共享来启动会话），然后生成一些密钥集。访问权限由用户密钥处理，您可以拥有只读用户，也可以撤销用户的实时协作访问权限。

children.author: inanutshellus
children.textRawHtml:
The concept that's MAGICAL in Reveal.js is vertical slides.

e.g. https://revealjs.com/#/2/1 <-- from here you can go up or down, then go right to continue the presentation.

You just make one slide deck with lightweight high-abstraction slides and depending on the audience you just... hit the down arrow. bam. now you're inside the tech slides. no nerdy tech guys? sweet, don't hit down on slide 3. Instead you're with the marketing guys, so hit down on slide 5.

Anyway - looks like you got rid of that in order to mimic Powerpoint, but... don't forget it's there. it's my favorite part of using Reveal.js!
children.textZhHtml:
Reveal.js 中最神奇的概念是垂直幻灯片。

例如https://revealjs.com/#/2/1 <-- 从这里您可以向上或向下，然后向右继续演示。

您只需使用轻量级高抽象幻灯片制作一组幻灯片，然后根据您的受众...点击向下箭头。砰。现在您已经进入了技术幻灯片。没有书呆子技术人员吗？太好了，不要点击幻灯片 3。相反，您与营销人员在一起，所以点击幻灯片 5。

无论如何 - 看起来您为了模仿 Powerpoint 而删除了它，但是...不要忘记它在那里。这是我最喜欢使用 Reveal.js 的部分！

children.author: gcanyon
children.textRawHtml: I guess I get the clarity (of vertical slides as you describe), but I'm wondering if two dimensions is limiting -- slides could be a flow chart, with various forks/side quests along the way. Obviously it would be possible to build something as confusing as the Colossal Cave, but the vertical slides use case would easily be a subset of that larger functionality, and it would leave it up to the user to decide their level of need for/tolerance of complexity.
children.textZhHtml: 我想我明白了（如您所描述的垂直幻灯片），但我想知道二维是否受到限制 - 幻灯片可能是一个流程图，一路上有各种分叉/支线任务。显然，构建像巨大洞穴一样令人困惑的东西是可能的，但垂直幻灯片用例很容易成为该更大功能的子集，并且将由用户决定他们对复杂性的需求/容忍程度。

children.author: starfallg

当我只处理单个文件时，我看不到如何存在 CDRT。我试图找出服务器在哪里。那么——这适合所有人吗？您是否担心您的 Cloudflare 帐户会因此受到攻击？

children.author: rpdillon
children.textRawHtml:
My project for the past few years is called AppDeck, collection of self-contained HTML files that know how to save themselves back to the AppDeck server, which is just WebDAV. AI has been enormously helpful in building out a suite of apps that can do this. So far I have a diagram editor that can produce SVGs, a multi-document editor that backs everything with markdown, but supports Kandan boards, documents, logs, and calendars. I also have a work flowy clone that I've really been enjoying organizing ideas with at work. There's a gallery app that inlines all of the images and can display them full screen in a light box and with a timed slideshow that also supports captions and encryption.

All of it just runs out of a folder on disk and can either be deployed locally or on a server behind a login. I intend to open source it, but I'm not quite proud enough yet of some of the implementation aspects to do so. Hope to do it this summer though!
children.textZhHtml:
过去几年我的项目称为 AppDeck，这是一组独立的 HTML 文件，这些文件知道如何将自己保存回 AppDeck 服务器，即 WebDAV。人工智能对于构建一套可以做到这一点的应用程序非常有帮助。到目前为止，我有一个可以生成 SVG 的图表编辑器，一个支持 Markdown 的所有内容的多文档编辑器，但支持 Kandan 看板、文档、日志和日历。我还有一个工作流畅的克隆，我非常喜欢在工作中用它来组织想法。有一个图库应用程序可以内嵌所有图像，并可以在灯箱中全屏显示它们，并带有定时幻灯片，还支持字幕和加密。

所有这些都只是在磁盘上的一个文件夹中运行，并且可以部署在本地或登录后的服务器上。我打算将其开源，但我对某些实现方面还不够自豪。希望今年夏天能做到！

children.author: Mtinie
children.textRawHtml: My initial reaction was “woah, you just described HyperCard”. Which isn’t exactly true in retrospect, but kudos for capturing the promise of (at least for me) one of the formative technologies that subtly, and not so subtly, influenced the web we know today.
children.textZhHtml: 我最初的反应是“哇哦，你刚刚描述了 HyperCard”。回想起来，这并不完全正确，但值得称赞的是，我抓住了一项形成性技术的希望（至少对我来说），它微妙地（而不是那么微妙地）影响了我们今天所知道的网络。

Again, very cool project! :)

如果两个用户同时在同一个项目中工作，这可能会很烦人。

再说一遍，非常酷的项目！ :)

children.author: moontear
children.textRawHtml:
How do we UPDATE the underlying engine once it gets new features? Since this is a self contained HTML file I am not sure how this works.

Editing multiple things at once (select via CTRL) needs some more options (like text centering).
children.textZhHtml:
一旦底层引擎获得新功能，我们如何更新它？由于这是一个独立的 HTML 文件，我不确定它是如何工作的。

一次编辑多个内容（通过 CTRL 选择）需要更多选项（例如文本居中）。

children.author: starfallg
children.textRawHtml: Bento has auto-update baked in. It checks for signed updates in a manifest, downloads it, rewrite the file structure with the new and reloads with the new file.
children.textZhHtml: Bento 内置了自动更新功能。它会检查清单中是否有已签名的更新，下载它，用新文件重写文件结构，然后用新文件重新加载。
